title: Slow-Roast Shoulder of Lamb
subtitle: Seven-hour shoulder that pulls apart with a fork
excerpt: A whole lamb shoulder slow-roasted at 160°C for six to seven hours is an entirely different thing from a roast leg. There is no carving: the collagen breaks down completely and the meat collapses into yielding, fatty chunks that serve themselves with a fork and spoon. Plan the time, do almost no work, and arrive at a table-centrepiece that needs nothing beyond flatbreads and a sharp green salad.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and Mediterranean slow-roast shoulder method. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: shoulder of mutton stewing references. Nigel Slater, Appetite (2001): the low-and-slow shoulder approach for pulled-style serving.

recipe.freezeNotes: Pulled shoulder freezes well in airtight containers for up to 3 months. Reheat covered with foil at 160°C until piping through.
recipe.batchNotes: A second shoulder can sit alongside in a second roasting pan. The oven time is fixed regardless of quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The shoulder can be cooked a day ahead and refrigerated in its juices. Reheat covered at 160°C for 45 minutes. The flavour deepens overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: The conversion of collagen (a tough connective tissue protein) into gelatin at temperatures above 70°C over long cooking times, which gives slow-cooked meat its tender, pull-apart texture and enriches the cooking liquid.

content.text: A shoulder of lamb is threaded with fat and connective tissue that a short roast would leave tough and chewy. Given time at a low temperature, that same connective tissue undergoes

and the shoulder becomes something altogether different: fibres that separate at a fork's pressure, fat that has melted through the meat rather than sitting on top of it, and pan juices thick with gelatin that need no thickening agent.

Six to seven hours at 160°C (140°C fan) is the standard. The shoulder does not need liquid in the pan: the fat and the moisture in the meat are sufficient. Covering tightly with foil for the first four hours keeps steam in; removing it for the last two hours crisps the surface. Pull it apart at the table with two forks.
A deep roasting pan is essential: the shoulder produces a large volume of fat and juice over seven hours and a shallow tin will spill. Heavy-duty foil for sealing tightly.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Using a paring knife, score the fat cap in a diamond pattern down to the meat. Make 18 deep incisions all over the shoulder and push a sliver of {{garlic}} of garlic and a rosemary piece into each cut. Rub with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, then press {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}} into all surfaces.

Place the halved {{onion}} onions cut-side down in the roasting pan. Set the shoulder fat-side up on top. Seal the pan tightly with a double layer of foil, crimping around the edges. Roast for 4 hours without opening the oven.

Remove the foil. The meat should be pulling away from the bone and the surface soft and pale. Continue roasting uncovered for a further 2 to 3 hours until the surface is deeply golden and the bone lifts free with almost no resistance. The total time is 6 to 7 hours depending on the shoulder's size and your oven.

Transfer to a board and rest for 20 minutes under foil. Use two forks to pull the meat apart directly at the table or in a serving dish. Spoon the pan juices over. Pour off the excess fat from the pan (save it for roasting potatoes) before spooning over the darker meat juices that settle below.

rows.symptom: Surface pale and soft at the end of the uncovered phase
rows.cause: Oven temperature drifted low, or the uncovered phase was not long enough
rows.fix: Raise to 180°C (160°C fan) for a final 30 minutes to colour the surface.

rows.symptom: Meat still tight and not pulling apart at 6 hours
rows.cause: Shoulder is large (over 2.5 kg) or the oven runs cool
rows.fix: Continue for another hour and check again. A 2.5 kg shoulder may need 8 hours.

rows.symptom: Pan juices too fatty and greasy
rows.cause: All the fat has rendered but not been skimmed
rows.fix: Tilt the pan and spoon off the clear fat layer before serving. The dark meat juices beneath are the sauce.

rows.symptom: Foil seal breaking during the covered phase
rows.cause: Insufficient crimping around the pan edge
rows.fix: Use two sheets of foil overlapped and crimped tightly. A gap allows steam to escape and the meat dries out.

content.text: The slow shoulder sits between the Sunday roast and the Middle Eastern slow-cooked lamb traditions that arrived in Britain during the twentieth century. It requires neither carving skills nor a precise thermometer reading: the result announces itself when the shoulder collapses under the weight of a fork. The pan juices are intensely savoury and need no augmentation. Serve with flatbreads to mop them up, a cucumber and yoghurt salad alongside, and roast vegetables if the oven has room.
